ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Kueh Rose; mapped to the closest parent class 'deep-fried snack food' (FOODON_03315091). A more specific term does not exist in the current FoodOn release.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for Kueh Rose. A0EJW (biscuits and cookies) is the closest defensible category anchor given the wheat-flour, egg, sugar, deep-fried composition.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept found for Kueh Rose or its parent category of deep-fried rose-shaped cracker.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Kueh Rose is approximately 5 small pieces (about 30 g), commonly consumed as a festive snack or teatime treat.

nutrient_source.title: Kueh Rose — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Kueh Rose (CR ID: F11080030). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken from this canonical HPB record. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for rosette cookies (USDA SR Legacy #18133) as HPB does not report fatty acid fractions; this adaptation is documented in notes.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Wheat-Flour Based Deep-Fried Snack Cookies — analogous estimate based on plain sweet biscuits/cookies (GI ~65)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Kueh Rose exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 65 (medium) is estimated by analogy to plain sweet wheat-flour cookies and biscuits listed in the University of Sydney GI Database, which report GI values of 59–70 for similar deep-fried or baked wheat-flour sweet snacks. Glycemic load per 30 g serving = (65 × 18.69 g available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = 12.1.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sugar content (25.3 g/100 g) with low dietary fibre (1.0 g/100 g) drives insulin-mediated sodium retention; deep-frying adds oxidised fats that contribute to arterial stiffness
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid Kueh Rose: the 25.3 g/100 g sugar load with only 1.0 g/100 g fibre promotes insulin-mediated sodium retention, and the deep-fried fat fraction contributes to vascular inflammation and arterial stiffness independent of the 130 mg/100 g sodium content.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar at 25.3 g/100 g exceeds the 20 g/100 g diabetes hard threshold; combined with 62.3 g/100 g refined carbohydrate and only 1.0 g/100 g dietary fibre, this food causes rapid and severe postprandial glucose excursions
type2_diabetes.guidance: Kueh Rose is contraindicated for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes: the 25.3 g/100 g sugar content far exceeds safe limits, the 62.3 g/100 g refined carbohydrate load with negligible fibre (1.0 g/100 g) produces severe postprandial hyperglycaemia, and the medium GI of 65 with a glycemic load of 12.1 per 30 g serving confirms clinically significant glucose impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High energy density (496 kcal/100 g), significant fat content (24.4 g/100 g including approximately 5.2 g/100 g saturated fat), very high sugar (25.3 g/100 g), and deep-frying-derived oxidised lipids collectively elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid Kueh Rose: the combination of 24.4 g/100 g total fat (approximately 5.2 g/100 g saturated), 25.3 g/100 g sugar, 496 kcal/100 g energy density, and deep-frying-generated oxidised lipids and advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) collectively promote dyslipidaemia, endothelial dysfunction, and atherosclerotic progression.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates pro-inflammatory AGEs (hard RA disqualifier); sugar at 25.3 g/100 g exceeds the 15 g/100 g RA threshold; saturated fat further promotes systemic inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Kueh Rose is unsuitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis: deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that directly worsen joint inflammation, sugar at 25.3 g/100 g exceeds the 15 g/100 g RA threshold, and the saturated fat content amplifies systemic pro-inflammatory signalling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kueh Rose (also known as Kuih Ros, Rosette Cookie, or Rose Cookie) is a traditional deep-fried festive snack popular in Singapore, Malaysia, and across the Malay Archipelago. It is made from a thin batter of wheat flour, egg, sugar, and water, which is dipped onto a specially shaped iron mould (typically in a rose or star pattern) and submerged in hot oil until the batter crisps and releases from the mould, forming a delicate, hollow, flower-shaped cracker. The result is a light, airy, and extremely crispy snack with a mildly sweet flavour and a golden-brown colour. Kueh Rose is commonly prepared during festive occasions such as Hari Raya Aidilfitri, Chinese New Year, and Deepavali, reflecting its cross-cultural popularity in Singapore. Nutritionally, Kueh Rose is energy-dense at 496 kcal per 100 g, with 24.4 g fat, 62.3 g carbohydrate, 25.3 g sugar, 6.7 g protein, and only 1.0 g dietary fibre per 100 g. The very high sugar and refined carbohydrate content with negligible fibre makes it clinically unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that are pro-inflammatory, making it also unsuitable for rheumatoid arthritis. The glycemic index is estimated at 65 (medium), yielding a glycemic load of approximately 12.1 per 30 g serving. Despite its festive cultural significance, Kueh Rose should be consumed only occasionally and in small portions by the general healthy population.
common_user_queries: Is rosette cookie the same as kueh rose?
Can I eat kueh rose if I have high blood pressure?
What is kueh rose made of?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values are sourced directly from the canonical HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID: F11080030, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat is adapted from USDA SR Legacy rosette cookie entry (#18133) and documented. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for similar wheat-flour sweet cookies, which is the approved GI source. All mandatory multilingual names for a Singaporean dish are present.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (5.2 g/100 g) is adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry #18133 (Rosette, cookie) as the HPB database does not report fatty acid fractions for this item. This adaptation is documented per source policy. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for Kueh Rose are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2026. 3. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on plain sweet wheat-flour cookies in the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Kueh Rose exists in any approved source. 4. The input record description referenced 'rice flour' in the health_suitability section, but the canonical HPB record and food description confirm the primary ingredient is wheat flour; the rice flour reference in the input health notes appears to be an error and has been corrected. 5. Halal status is assessed as likely halal based on ingredients (wheat flour, egg, sugar, water, vegetable oil) with no pork or alcohol components; formal halal certification status is not confirmed in the HPB record.
